subject: Image warp model for tiltseries in M
body:
Hi Dimitry, hi all,

TLDR:

1. What is (or are) the reference image(s) that the image warp model is 
defined for?
2. How does the geometry of image warp model relate to untransformed tilt 
images and the tomogram (and by extension the aligned tilt series).

---
Here's what I mean (my apologies if this should have been clear to me from 
the paper/documentation):
I am trying to understand the image deformation model implemented in M and 
having a hard time understanding how its geometry is related to the 
untransformed tilt images and the tomogram.

>From the M paper and the user guide, I gather that, for tiltseries data, 
the "image warp" model is akin to a stack of bicubic spline grids where 
each layer in the stack defines the deformation in one tilt image. The user 
guide's suggestion to use 6x4 image warp grids for K3 data sounded like the 
image warp grid is based on the geometry of the untransformed tilt images 
(i.e not rotated or translated to align the tilt axis to the y-axis). What 
I am unsure about is what the base/reference image is that the image 
deformation is defined for? 

To be clear, I don't mean how the deformation grid is calculated, but 
rather what it is defined for in the form it is stored in the 
<tomogram>.xml files at GridMovementX and Y nodes. Looking at the numbers, 
it looks like they describe the displacement of each patch center (in 
Angstroms?) in the image warp grid and can be fit on a 2D cubic spline 
function to generate a deformation field. But, what image would this 
deformation field be applied to (even if it isn't actually ever applied to 
a whole image, I want to understand what it would make sense to apply to)?

I could think of two scenarios, both of which I am a relatively unsure 
about.

Scenario 1. *The deformation is defined for the unrotated tilt averages* 
(likely not the case): This would mean that each layer in image warp model 
contains deformations that would be applied to one tilt image. This would 
make sense why the shape of the deformation grid is similar to the image 
(i.e. 6x4 grid for 6K x 4K data) and has per tilt temporal resolution. But 
since the imaged area in X and Y changes between different tilt angles 
(i.e. you see much larger area at higher tilts, for most of which you don't 
have particles picked on and therefore no way to get a deformation model), 
the warp grid would also need to be expanded and compressed based on the 
tilt angle to be limited to the area for which a deformation model can be 
generate, which I don't think is happening (at least couldn't find the 
evidence of in the papers or the metadata files).

Scenario 2. *The deformation defined for a reference projection: *This 
would mean that the reference image is same for all tilts (say projection 
of the tomogram at 0 deg tilt), and the each bicubic spline would describe 
the deformations to be applied to the reference image to get the updated 
projection of the sample and particle positions for each time point (tilt). 
So, the deformation grid can always have the same shape regardless of the 
tilt angle, because it is always defined for one reference image. I feel 
like this is close to the truth, but what exactly *is* the reference image? 
My guess would have been the projection of the tomogram along the Z axis 
(at 0 deg tilt), but this projection would be rotated w.r.t. the original 
tilt images. So, the choice the grid dimensions would actually also be 
influenced by the tilt-axis rotation. i.e. 4x6 patches if tilt-axis is 
close to +-90 deg (like is the case for volume warp). Yet, the M wiki 
suggests following the shape of the unrotated images while setting up the 
image warp grid, which implies that the reference image has the same shape 
as the unrotated tilt images independent of the tilt axis rotation. So, I 
am stuck.

Thanks for bearing with the details. I would really appreciate some help 
clarifying this.

Best,
Arjun
